title: The Chancellorship: Consolidation of Power 1933–1939
summary: The six years between Hitler's appointment as Chancellor and the outbreak of war constituted the most rapid destruction of a constitutional state in modern history.

sections.heading: Appointment and Initial Cabinet, January 1933
sections.text: On 30 January 1933, President Paul von Hindenburg appointed Hitler Chancellor of a coalition cabinet in which the NSDAP held only three of eleven positions — Hitler as Chancellor, Frick as Interior Minister, and Göring as Minister Without Portfolio. Hindenburg and his adviser Franz von Papen believed Hitler could be 'contained' and used as a political tool by the conservative establishment. This assessment proved catastrophically wrong within months. Hitler moved immediately to exploit his constitutional position, calling new elections and using the emergency powers latent in the Weimar constitution.

sections.heading: The Reichstag Fire and the Emergency Decrees, February–March 1933
sections.text: On the night of 27–28 February 1933, the Reichstag building was set ablaze. A Dutch communist, Marinus van der Lubbe, was arrested at the scene and executed for the crime. Whether the Nazis engineered the fire, allowed it to happen, or merely exploited van der Lubbe's genuinely solitary action remains historically debated; the German communist party showed no foreknowledge. What is certain is the Nazi response: within 24 hours, Hindenburg signed the Reichstag Fire Decree (Verordnung des Reichspräsidenten zum Schutz von Volk und Staat), which suspended the fundamental civil liberties guaranteed by the Weimar constitution — freedom of speech, press, assembly, and privacy of communications. The decree was never repealed. It formed the legal framework for the terror that followed.

sections.heading: The Enabling Act: End of Parliamentary Democracy, March 1933
sections.text: In the elections of 5 March 1933, held under conditions of SA intimidation and the suspension of civil liberties, the NSDAP won 43.9% of the vote — a plurality but not a majority. To secure the two-thirds majority needed for constitutional amendment, Hitler needed the Centre Party and other smaller parties. By promising to respect the Concordat with the Catholic Church and other assurances (not honoured), he secured their votes. On 23 March 1933, the Reichstag passed the Enabling Act (Ermächtigungsgesetz) by 444 votes to 94 — only the Social Democrats voted against; the Communist deputies had already been arrested or fled. The Act gave Hitler's cabinet full legislative authority for four years, bypassing both the Reichstag and President Hindenburg. Parliamentary democracy in Germany was over.

sections.heading: Gleichschaltung: Coordination of German Society, 1933–1934
sections.text: The term Gleichschaltung — 'coordination' or 'bringing into line' — describes the process by which the Nazis systematically brought all institutions of German life under party control. Trade unions were dissolved in May 1933 and replaced by the Deutsche Arbeitsfront. Political parties were banned — first the SPD and KPD, then the centre-right parties dissolved themselves under pressure. A law of 14 July 1933 made the NSDAP the sole legal political party. State parliaments were dissolved and replaced by Reich governors. The civil service, judiciary, media, and cultural life were purged of Jews and political opponents by the Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service (April 1933). By the end of 1933, no independent institutional counterweight to the Nazi party remained.

sections.heading: The Night of the Long Knives and the Death of Hindenburg, 1934
sections.text: Ernst Röhm, the SA commander, was pushing for a 'second revolution' — absorbing the Wehrmacht into an SA-dominated 'people's army'. This alarmed both the military, whose professional status would be threatened, and Hitler's conservative allies, who feared social radicalism. Between 30 June and 2 July 1934 — the Night of the Long Knives — the SS and Gestapo murdered at least 85 people, including Röhm, former Chancellor Schleicher, and various other perceived threats. The killings were subsequently legalised retroactively. On 2 August 1934, Hindenburg died. Within hours, Hitler merged the offices of Chancellor and President, creating the title Führer und Reichskanzler. The Wehrmacht swore a personal oath of loyalty to Hitler by name — a decision that would bind the officer corps throughout the war.

sections.heading: Rearmament and the Four-Year Plan
sections.text: In contravention of the Treaty of Versailles, Germany began open rearmament in March 1935 with the announcement of conscription and the re-established Luftwaffe. The Anglo-German Naval Agreement of June 1935 gave the expansion partial international legitimacy. In 1936, Hitler appointed Göring to direct the Four-Year Plan, an economic programme designed to make Germany self-sufficient in war materials within four years. Industrial output was reoriented toward armaments; consumer goods production was deliberately suppressed. By 1938, Germany was spending 17% of GNP on military expenditure — a level unsustainable without eventual expansion and plunder.

sections.heading: Territorial Expansion Without War, 1936–1939
sections.text: Hitler's foreign policy in the pre-war years produced successive triumphs: the remilitarisation of the Rhineland (March 1936), unopposed; the Anschluss with Austria (March 1938), unopposed; the Sudetenland crisis resolved in Germany's favour at Munich (September 1938), with British and French consent; and the occupation of rump Czechoslovakia (March 1939), which Britain and France protested but did not resist. Each success reinforced Hitler's conviction that the Western powers lacked the will to fight, and that his own intuition — repeatedly vindicated over his generals' caution — was infallible. The destruction of Czechoslovakia was the point at which British policy finally hardened: Chamberlain's government extended military guarantees to Poland in March 1939.

sections.heading: Persecution of Jews: The Legal Phase, 1933–1938
sections.text: In the period before the war, antisemitic persecution was pursued principally through legal and economic means. The April 1933 boycott of Jewish businesses was followed by the Law for the Restoration of the Professional Civil Service (expelling Jews from public employment), the Nuremberg Laws (September 1935, stripping Jews of citizenship and prohibiting Jewish-German marriage), and progressive exclusion from professions, universities, and public life. The November 1938 Kristallnacht pogrom — during which over 7,500 Jewish businesses were destroyed, 1,400 synagogues burned, and 30,000 Jews arrested and sent to camps — marked the shift toward outright physical violence. The proceeds of the pogrom were effectively stolen by the state: Jews were required to pay a 1-billion-Reichsmark 'atonement fine' for the damage done to their own property.

sources.ref: Evans, Richard J. The Third Reich in Power. Penguin Press, 2005.
Kershaw, Ian. Hitler: 1889–1936 Hubris. W.W. Norton, 1998. Chapters 12–16.
Gellately, Robert. Backing Hitler: Consent and Coercion in Nazi Germany. Oxford University Press, 2001.
Burleigh, Michael. The Third Reich: A New History. Hill and Wang, 2000.
